content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 14 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 14 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 14:1 — "Pursue love" / "Pursue the love" / "Let love be your highest goal!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Pursue the love" | Literal with article ten agapen |
| LOGOS | "Pursue love" | Standard English (drops the article) |
| PNEUMA | "Let love be your highest goal!" | Dynamic paraphrase with exclamation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Pursue love" (LOGOS). EMET's article ("the love") is grammatically present in Greek but unnecessary in English. PNEUMA's "highest goal" shifts the metaphor from pursuit (active verb) to goal-setting (passive framing), and the exclamation mark adds emphasis not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:2 — "no one understands him" / "no one hears" / "people won't be able to understand you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "no one hears" | Literal akouei — "hears" (in the sense of comprehends) |
| LOGOS | "no one understands him" | Interprets akouei as "understands" |
| PNEUMA | "people won't be able to understand you" | Dynamic expansion with second person |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "no one understands him" (LOGOS). The Greek akouei here means "understands/comprehends" (cf. the English "I hear you" meaning "I understand you"). EMET's "hears" could be misread as "no one is listening" rather than "no one comprehends."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:3 — "upbuilding and encouragement and consolation" / "building up and encouragement and consolation" / "strengthens others, encourages them, and comforts them"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "building up and encouragement and consolation" | Literal oikodomen, paraklesin, paramythian |
| LOGOS | "upbuilding and encouragement and consolation" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "strengthens others, encourages them, and comforts them" | Dynamic with added objects |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The three-noun structure is a Pauline triad that should be preserved as nouns, not converted to verbs (PNEUMA). "Upbuilding" preserves the architectural metaphor of oikodome.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:9 — "speaking into the air" / "speaking into [the] air" / "talking into empty space"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "speaking into [the] air" | Literal eis aera lalontes |
| LOGOS | "speaking into the air" | Same rendering |
| PNEUMA | "You might as well be talking into empty space" | Dynamic paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. All three convey the same idea, but PNEUMA's "empty space" modernizes the image unnecessarily. "Speaking into the air" is a vivid and readily understood idiom in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:11 — "a foreigner" / "a barbarian" / "a foreigner"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a barbarian" | Literal barbaros |
| LOGOS | "a foreigner" | Contextual rendering |
| PNEUMA | "a foreigner" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a foreigner" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Greek barbaros meant "one who speaks an unintelligible language" (onomatopoeia for incomprehensible speech: "bar-bar"). "Barbarian" in modern English connotes uncivilized violence, which is not Paul's meaning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 14:12 — "manifestations of the Spirit" / "spirits" / "the special abilities the Spirit gives"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "zealots of spirits" | Literal zelotai pneumaton |
| LOGOS | "eager for manifestations of the Spirit" | Interprets pneumaton as Spirit-manifestations |
| PNEUMA | "so eager to have the special abilities the Spirit gives" | Dynamic expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "zealots of spirits" is transparent to the Greek but confusing in English. LOGOS's interpretation of pneumaton as "manifestations of the Spirit" is contextually justified. PNEUMA's paraphrase adds too much.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:16 — "anyone in the position of an outsider" / "the [one] filling the place of the uninstructed" / "those who don't understand you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] filling the place of the uninstructed" | Literal ton topon tou idiotou |
| LOGOS | "anyone in the position of an outsider" | Renders idiotes as "outsider" |
| PNEUMA | "those who don't understand you" | Dynamic simplification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The idiotes is someone who lacks specialized knowledge — a "layperson" or "outsider" to the practice. EMET's "uninstructed" is accurate but cumbersome. PNEUMA's "don't understand you" loses the social/positional sense.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:20 — "Be infants in evil, but in your thinking be mature" / "in the evil be infants, and in the thinking become mature" / "Be innocent as babies when it comes to evil, but be mature in understanding"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the evil be infants, and in the thinking become mature" | Literal word order |
| LOGOS | "Be infants in evil, but in your thinking be mature" | Reordered for English |
| PNEUMA | "Be innocent as babies when it comes to evil, but be mature in understanding matters of this kind" | Dynamic expansion; "innocent as babies" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "innocent as babies" adds a simile, and "matters of this kind" narrows the scope. LOGOS preserves the contrast concisely.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:23 — "out of your minds" / "mad" / "crazy"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you are mad" | Literal mainesthe — "you are mad/raving" |
| LOGOS | "you are out of your minds" | Idiomatic English |
| PNEUMA | "they will think you are crazy" | Colloquial "crazy" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "out of your minds" (LOGOS). EMET's "mad" is somewhat archaic. PNEUMA's "crazy" is too colloquial for the register of the consensus text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:25 — "the secrets of his heart are disclosed" / "the hidden [things] of the heart of him become manifest" / "their secret thoughts will be exposed"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the hidden [things] of the heart of him become manifest" | Literal ta krypta tes kardias |
| LOGOS | "the secrets of his heart are disclosed" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "their secret thoughts will be exposed" | Plural subject; "exposed" is stronger |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "exposed" has a negative connotation that the Greek phanera ("made manifest/disclosed") does not necessarily carry. The disclosure leads to worship, not shame.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:26 — "a hymn, a lesson, a revelation, a tongue, or an interpretation" / "a psalm, has a teaching, has a revelation, has a tongue, has an interpretation" / expanded list

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "each has a psalm, has a teaching, has a revelation, has a tongue, has an interpretation" | Literal with repeated "has" |
| LOGOS | "each one has a hymn, a lesson, a revelation, a tongue, or an interpretation" | "Hymn" for psalmon; "lesson" for didachen |
| PNEUMA | "one will sing, another will teach, another will tell some special revelation God has given, one will speak in tongues, and another will interpret" | Full expansion with different subjects |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's repeated "has" is faithful to the Greek structure. PNEUMA distributes the gifts among different people, which changes the meaning — the Greek says each person has one of these contributions, not that different people have different ones. LOGOS's "hymn" for psalmon is preferable to EMET's "psalm" (which implies a biblical psalm rather than a worship song).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:33-34 — "As in all the churches of the saints, the women should keep silent" — clause division

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | v.33: "for God is not [a God] of disorder but of peace. As in all the assemblies of the holy [ones]," / v.34: "let the women be silent" | Attaches "as in all the assemblies" to v.34 |
| LOGOS | v.33: "For God is not a God of confusion but of peace. As in all the churches of the saints," / v.34: "the women should keep silent" | Same clause division as EMET |
| PNEUMA | v.33: "For God is not a God of disorder but of peace, as in all the meetings of God's holy people." / v.34: "Women should be silent" | Attaches "as in all the meetings" to v.33 |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's clause division, attaching "As in all the churches of the saints" to the beginning of v.34. This is a textually debated punctuation issue. The clause can modify either what precedes (God is a God of peace, as in all churches) or what follows (as in all churches, women should be silent). The SOPHIA consensus follows the traditional division, which connects the silence instruction to universal church practice.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/EMET. PNEUMA's alternative division is noted as textually defensible.

---

## 14:34 — "churches" / "assemblies" / "church meetings"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "assemblies" | Renders ekklesia as "assembly" |
| LOGOS | "churches" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "church meetings" | Interprets as gathered meetings |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "churches" (LOGOS). PNEUMA's "church meetings" narrows ekklesia to the gathering event, whereas Paul's language encompasses the church community as a whole.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:38 — "he is not recognized" / "he is ignored" / "you yourself will not be recognized"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if anyone is ignorant, he is ignored" | Wordplay: agnoei/agnoeitai |
| LOGOS | "If anyone does not recognize this, he is not recognized" | Preserves the verbal echo |
| PNEUMA | "if you do not recognize this, you yourself will not be recognized" | Second person; adds "yourself" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Both EMET and LOGOS attempt to preserve the Greek wordplay (agnoei/agnoeitai — "does not know/is not known"). EMET's "ignorant/ignored" captures it well. LOGOS's rendering is slightly clearer. PNEUMA shifts to second person, which changes the generalizing force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 14:40 — "decently and in order" / "properly and in order" / "properly and in order"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "properly and in order" | Literal euschemonos kai kata taxin |
| LOGOS | "decently and in order" | Traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "properly and in order" | Same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "decently and in order" (LOGOS). This is the traditional and widely recognized rendering of this summary verse.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA expansion/paraphrase | 7 | Dynamic recasting of concise Greek, added exclamation marks, expanded subjects |
| EMET literalism | 5 | "Barbaros," "zealots of spirits," repeated "has," article retention |
| Clause division (vv.33-34) | 1 | Significant structural disagreement about where to attach "as in all the churches" |
| Register/tone | 3 | "Mad" vs. "out of your minds" vs. "crazy"; "decently" vs. "properly" |
| Structural preservation | 2 | Noun triad (v.3), worship-element list (v.26) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 14 produced a particularly notable structural disagreement at vv.33-34, where the clause division affects the meaning of the controversial silence passage. PNEUMA's consistent tendency to expand and paraphrase was again evident throughout, particularly in the worship-element list (v.26) where PNEUMA distributed gifts among different speakers rather than preserving Paul's point that each person contributes one element. The consensus consistently favored LOGOS's balance of accuracy, tradition, and readability.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
